heuristics.rule: Never name a salary number first — the first number anchors the entire negotiation
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter or HR form asks you to state your salary expectations or current compensation
heuristics.action: Deflect with 'I'm more focused on fit right now — if we're right for each other, we can be flexible on numbers' or enter $1 on forms to proceed
heuristics.because: Whoever names first sets the ceiling for their own outcome; if you name low, the company accepts and you leave money on the table; if you name high without knowing their range, you may anchor awkwardly

heuristics.rule: Only negotiate compensation after the company has reached 'Yes-If' status — not while still in 'No-But' evaluation
heuristics.condition: When you are still in interview or evaluation stages and a recruiter tries to discuss compensation
heuristics.action: Defer with 'I look forward to receiving your offer' — signal that comp discussion comes after the hiring decision, not during it
heuristics.because: Negotiating while the company is still deciding whether to hire you forces excessive compromise; once they've decided they want you, leverage shifts substantially in your favor

heuristics.rule: Treat negotiation as a fixed rule, not a situational choice — always counter at least once
heuristics.condition: When any job offer arrives, regardless of how generous it seems
heuristics.action: Counter or request at least one adjustment before accepting
heuristics.because: The worst-case outcome of negotiating is receiving the original offer unchanged; upside exists, downside does not — making it a pure expected-value positive action every time

heuristics.rule: When salary hits a ceiling, pivot immediately to signing bonus or equity
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter signals that base salary cannot move due to pay bands or HR constraints
heuristics.action: Say 'If you could increase the signing bonus to X, I could accept this salary' — reframe the ask to a one-time cost
heuristics.because: Companies have asymmetric budget flexibility; signing bonuses don't affect ongoing payroll equity or pay-band precedent, making them easier for employers to grant while giving you equivalent real value

heuristics.rule: Proclaim a specific reason for every negotiation request to dramatically increase acceptance rate
heuristics.condition: When making any counter-request on salary, bonus, equity, or other terms
heuristics.action: Attach a concrete justification — student loans, relocation costs, market data — even if the reason is modest
heuristics.because: Reasons make requests feel human and legitimate rather than greedy; humans respond to narrative context, and justified requests are psychologically easier for recruiters to approve internally

heuristics.rule: Explicitly call out exploding offer deadlines — never let them silently expire
heuristics.condition: When an offer comes with a 24–72 hour expiration window
heuristics.action: Directly state 'This timeline doesn't work for me — I need time to make an informed decision and consult with my family'
heuristics.because: Virtually every exploding offer extends when called out explicitly; but silently assuming they'll still want you after expiry is a mistake — the extension only happens if you request it

heuristics.rule: Use an external decision-maker buffer to deflect immediate pressure
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter is pushing for a same-call decision or immediate commitment
heuristics.action: Say 'I'll review this and discuss with my family — I'll follow up if I have questions'
heuristics.because: Removes you from direct real-time pressure; the recruiter cannot demand an instant decision if the decision is not solely yours, buying you time without appearing uninterested

heuristics.rule: Reveal a quantified value proposition during negotiation to reframe the cost of your salary request
heuristics.condition: When you have a prior achievement that maps directly to the company's core business metric
heuristics.action: Quantify your past impact ('I increased sales 3% at my last company — what would 1% mean here?') and let them calculate before discussing your ask
heuristics.because: Once the employer anchors on a large value figure (e.g., millions in revenue), a $4,000–$10,000 salary increase becomes psychologically trivial by comparison

heuristics.rule: Contact other companies immediately when you receive a strong offer to compress timelines
heuristics.condition: When you receive an offer from one company while other processes are still ongoing
heuristics.action: Email other companies: 'I've received a strong offer and my timeline is compressed — can you expedite?'
heuristics.because: This is not manipulation — it naturally surfaces genuine interest from other parties and creates real competing leverage; companies respond rationally to competitive signals

heuristics.rule: Maintain visible enthusiasm throughout the negotiation, even when numbers disappoint
heuristics.condition: When an offer comes in lower than expected or the company pushes back on your counter
heuristics.action: Reiterate excitement for the mission and desire to make it work before restating your ask
heuristics.because: Companies fear candidates who 'go bad' during negotiations; if your enthusiasm visibly drops, you become less attractive at exactly the moment you need the most leverage

decision_factors.factor: Whether you have competing offers from companies you'd actually work for
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A real competing offer is the single most reliable source of negotiating leverage — it transforms your ask from opinion into market evidence and creates genuine optionality
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Count active offers or imminent offers from companies you would genuinely accept; distinguish real competing offers from vague 'other conversations'

decision_factors.factor: Timing overlap of multiple active offer windows
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Leverage from competing offers only exists if offer windows actually overlap; a competing offer that expired two weeks ago provides no real pressure
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Map interview start dates and typical decision timelines; start large-company interviews first (slower, wider windows) and startup interviews later (faster, tighter) to maximize overlap

decision_factors.factor: Whether the company has reached 'Yes-If' vs. 'No-But' status on hiring you
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Negotiating before the company has decided to hire you forces compromise; negotiating after they've committed gives you full leverage
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether you've received a formal or informal offer signal; 'we'd like to make you an offer' is Yes-If; 'we're still evaluating' is No-But

decision_factors.factor: Information asymmetry — what the company knows about your current salary and alternatives
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Your negotiating power is directly proportional to what the company does not know about your floor and alternatives; revealing your current salary or reservation price collapses that power
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Audit what you've disclosed: current comp, specific asks, how you rank this offer vs. others — each disclosure reduces asymmetry

decision_factors.factor: Which compensation dimensions have flexibility at this specific company
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Companies have asymmetric flexibility across comp components; salary may be locked by pay bands while signing bonuses, equity, and vacation are freely adjustable
decision_factors.how_to_assess: When salary hits resistance, ask 'What can you approve on your own?' to identify which levers remain open

decision_factors.factor: How differentiated your skills are relative to this company's core needs
decision_factors.why_it_matters: You are more valuable to companies where your specific skills are scarce and central; differentiation yields stronger offers and more negotiating room
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask 'What are the key projects defining this company's future, and how does someone get on those teams?' — assess fit between your strengths and their stated priorities

decision_factors.factor: How much the company has already invested in your interview process
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Sunk interview costs create strong pressure on the company to reach agreement; a company that has spent tens of thousands evaluating you is highly motivated not to lose you over modest salary differences
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Count interview rounds completed, number of interviewers involved, time elapsed — more investment means more pressure on their side

decision_factors.factor: The long-term compounding effect of the current negotiation outcome
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Each offer sets the baseline for future raises and the anchor for your next employer's offer; undercompensation compounds across decades
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate the 10-year gross value of a $5K salary difference; assess whether this role sets a comp baseline you want to carry forward

decision_factors.factor: Whether justifications for requests are market-based vs. personal-need-based
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Market data ('comparable roles pay X') is far more persuasive than personal circumstance ('I have high rent'); the type of justification affects acceptance rate
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Before negotiating, gather external compensation benchmarks (Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, Blind, peer offers) to anchor requests in market reality rather than personal need

decision_factors.factor: Candidate's visible enthusiasm and likelihood-to-sign signal
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Companies invest more negotiating effort in candidates they believe they can close; enthusiasm is itself a form of leverage when it signals genuine interest without desperation
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether your communication throughout the process has conveyed excitement about the specific company and role, not just about compensation

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Revealing your current salary when asked
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The company can now anchor your offer to your past underpayment rather than your market value; you've effectively justified a below-market offer by providing evidence that you've accepted below-market pay before
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who are currently underpaid relative to market, early-career professionals, and those switching from lower-paying industries or geographies

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting the first offer immediately without countering
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You signal that you either didn't know negotiation was possible or didn't believe you had standing to negotiate — which experienced hiring managers interpret as a competence signal about your professional self-awareness
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: First-time job seekers and engineers who have internalized the cultural norm that discussing compensation is unseemly

hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You leave significant value on the table in dimensions where companies have far more flexibility — signing bonuses, equity, vacation, professional development, remote work — while fighting hardest on the dimension most constrained by HR pay bands
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone whose salary ask hits a hard company ceiling but who doesn't know to redirect to other levers

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Letting an exploding offer deadline pass without explicitly challenging it
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The offer lapses; the assumption that 'they'll still want me' is almost always wrong because the company interprets silence as disinterest or indecisiveness, not as a negotiating move
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who are conflict-averse and assume the company will proactively extend without being asked

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Making a specific salary counter-request early in negotiation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You reveal your floor — the company now knows the minimum you'll accept and can offer just above it rather than their true ceiling
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who mistake specificity for confidence; particularly harms those who would have received higher offers if they had maintained information asymmetry

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating negotiation as a one-shot transaction focused only on the current offer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You optimize for the immediate package while ignoring that the number sets your raise baseline and your next employer's anchor; short-term framing systematically undervalues the compounding effect of early-career negotiations
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Early-career professionals who are focused on getting the offer rather than optimizing lifetime earnings

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using personal financial need as the primary justification for a higher salary
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The employer is not buying your expenses — they are buying your labor; need-based justifications are weak because they don't connect to your value, and they can create perception of financial desperation that weakens your leverage
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with genuine financial pressure (student loans, relocation costs, family obligations) who frame it as their primary argument rather than as a supporting detail alongside market data

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Fabricating or exaggerating a competing offer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Recruiters have access to detailed compensation benchmarks and professional networks; they can often detect implausible offers, and being caught in dishonesty permanently damages the relationship and can rescind the real offer
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with no real competing offers who feel pressure to manufacture leverage

failure_patterns.pattern: Naming a specific salary number before the company has made an offer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates interpret the question as a necessary formality or feel that providing a number demonstrates decisiveness and confidence
failure_patterns.consequence: Anchors the negotiation at the candidate's stated number rather than the company's ceiling; if the number is below what the company would have offered, the candidate has permanently reduced their own outcome
warning_signs: Feeling relieved to 'get the salary question out of the way' early
Treating the compensation question as a qualification screening step rather than a negotiation move

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting an offer on the spot during the verbal delivery call
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Excitement and relief override deliberate decision-making; candidates fear appearing ungrateful or difficult, and recruiters often imply that immediate acceptance is the expected response
failure_patterns.consequence: Signals that the candidate did not know negotiation was possible or expected; leaves money on the table across all negotiable dimensions without any attempt to improve terms
warning_signs: Saying 'Yes, absolutely!' before even hearing the full offer details
Not asking for the offer in writing before committing

failure_patterns.pattern: Revealing current compensation when asked without redirecting
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates assume the question is standard and that refusing to answer will harm their candidacy; the social pressure to answer direct questions overrides strategic awareness
failure_patterns.consequence: Provides the company an anchor for your offer based on your past underpayment rather than your market value; particularly costly for candidates currently earning below market
warning_signs: Treating HR's request for current salary as a required form field
Not having a prepared deflection response before the first recruiter call

failure_patterns.pattern: Negotiating with visible frustration or declining enthusiasm when numbers disappoint
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Genuine emotional reaction to a low offer; candidates conflate 'negotiating hard' with 'expressing disappointment' as a pressure tactic
failure_patterns.consequence: Makes the candidate less attractive at precisely the moment leverage is most needed; companies de-prioritize candidates who seem likely to accept unhappily or leave quickly
warning_signs: Responding to a low offer with a long silence followed by a flat tone
Statements like 'I was hoping for significantly more than that'

failure_patterns.pattern: Letting an exploding offer deadline lapse without explicitly requesting an extension
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Conflict-averse candidates assume that the company will proactively reach out or that their interest is self-evident; they also fear that challenging the deadline will signal wavering commitment
failure_patterns.consequence: Offer lapses; the candidate loses the option entirely when a single direct sentence would have extended the window in nearly every case
warning_signs: Thinking 'they must know I'm still interested' without having communicated it
Treating the deadline as a genuine signal about the company's culture rather than a standard pressure tactic

failure_patterns.pattern: Negotiating solely on salary and stopping when salary hits a ceiling
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates frame compensation mentally as 'salary' rather than 'total package'; when one number stops moving, they conclude negotiation is over
failure_patterns.consequence: Misses signing bonuses, equity grants, professional development budgets, extra vacation, flexible work arrangements — all of which the company may grant freely once salary is exhausted
warning_signs: Accepting 'our salary band is fixed' as the end of the conversation
Not having prepared a list of non-salary items before entering negotiation

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating negotiation as morally questionable or as 'haggling'
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Cultural conditioning, particularly among engineers, frames compensation discussion as unseemly; candidates internalize this as a virtue signal about being 'not money-motivated'
failure_patterns.consequence: Creates systematic and permanent undercompensation that compounds across raises, future offers, and decades of career earnings
warning_signs: Saying 'I don't want to seem greedy' when considering whether to counter
Feeling genuine discomfort or guilt when the recruiter pushes back on a counter-offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Using personal financial need rather than market data as the primary justification for a higher number
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates know their own expenses intimately and assume the employer will find them sympathetic; it feels honest and direct to explain personal circumstances
failure_patterns.consequence: Need-based justifications are weak because they're not connected to the candidate's labor value; they can also signal financial desperation, weakening leverage
warning_signs: Opening a counter with 'I have significant student loans' as the primary argument
Not having researched market compensation data before the negotiation call

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you received a formal or informal signal that the company wants to hire you — or are you still in evaluation?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you are in 'Yes-If' (post-hiring-decision) or 'No-But' (still being evaluated) status, which determines how much leverage you actually have
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What specific comp dimensions have they discussed — salary only, or total package including bonus and equity?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Defer all compensation discussion until you receive an offer signal; negotiating during evaluation forces premature compromise

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have any competing offers, or imminent offers from companies you would actually work for?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you have real market leverage or are negotiating purely on assertion; competing offers are the most powerful single source of negotiating power
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Do the offer windows actually overlap — is the competing offer still active right now?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are you actively interviewing elsewhere, and can you compress timelines to create overlap? If not, your leverage must come from other sources — quantified value propositions, market data, or demonstrated alternatives

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you disclosed your current salary or named a specific salary number at any point in this process?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether information asymmetry has already been compromised; if you've named a number, the negotiation is partially anchored and strategy must shift
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Was the number you named above or below what they've offered? If below, you need to reframe using market data rather than trying to walk back your stated floor
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Good — maintain that asymmetry; prepare deflection scripts for when they ask again

diagnostic_questions.question: When the company pushed back on your counter, what did you say and what was your tone?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is maintaining positive framing under pressure or letting frustration show; enthusiasm loss during negotiation is one of the most common and costly mistakes
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Did you reiterate excitement for the role before restating your ask, or did you lead with the counter?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you countered yet at all, or accepted the pushback as final?

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you negotiating only on base salary, or have you mapped out all the dimensions you could adjust?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate understands that negotiation is multi-dimensional and whether they know where company flexibility actually lives
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Which non-salary items have you already asked about — signing bonus, equity cliff, vacation, remote work, professional development budget?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Why not — do you believe those items aren't negotiable, or did salary seem like the highest-value target?

diagnostic_questions.question: Does this offer come with a deadline, and have you explicitly addressed it with the recruiter?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether there is an exploding offer dynamic in play, and whether the candidate knows they must explicitly request an extension rather than assuming it will be granted
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you stated clearly that the timeline doesn't work for you and asked for an extension?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: How much time do you have? Is this enough to run your decision process deliberately, or are you being implicitly pressured?

diagnostic_questions.question: What justification have you given or are you planning to give for your counter-request?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is using market-based framing (strong) or personal-need framing (weak), and whether they have a specific reason at all
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Is the reason tied to market data, your quantified impact, or personal circumstance? The first two are significantly more persuasive
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: You need a reason — requests without justification feel arbitrary; even a modest reason substantially increases acceptance rate

diagnostic_questions.question: How many interview rounds has this company put you through, and how many people have they involved?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: The company's sunk investment in your candidacy; more rounds and more interviewers means stronger pressure on their side to reach agreement, which is leverage you can use
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you internalized that they are highly motivated to close you — or are you still treating yourself as the supplicant in this transaction?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are you early in the process? If so, focus on establishing fit before any comp discussion

industry_realities.common_belief: Negotiating a job offer is rude, risky, or signals that you only care about money
industry_realities.actual_reality: Experienced hiring managers expect negotiation; candidates who accept immediately are often perceived as either inexperienced or unaware of their market value — both of which are competence signals that work against you
industry_realities.implication: Always counter at least once, framed positively; negotiation signals professional sophistication, not greed

industry_realities.common_belief: A salary increase of a few thousand dollars isn't worth the awkwardness of negotiating
industry_realities.actual_reality: A $5,000 salary increase compounded over 10 years is worth approximately $100,000 gross; it also sets the baseline for every future raise and the anchor for your next employer's offer
industry_realities.implication: Calculate the true long-term value before deciding a request is too small to make; the compounding effect makes early-career negotiations disproportionately important

industry_realities.common_belief: The company is doing you a favor by hiring you
industry_realities.actual_reality: By the time an offer is extended, the company has already invested tens of thousands of dollars in interviewing you and has decided they need you to solve a specific business problem; they are highly motivated not to lose you
industry_realities.implication: Enter negotiation understanding that you have substantial leverage — the company wants to close this deal as much as you do

industry_realities.common_belief: If you ask for more than they can give, they'll rescind the offer
industry_realities.actual_reality: The absolute worst realistic outcome of a reasonable counter-offer is receiving the original offer unchanged; companies do not rescind offers because candidates negotiated in good faith
industry_realities.implication: The asymmetry of outcomes (upside: more money; downside: original offer) makes negotiating always the correct expected-value decision

industry_realities.common_belief: The salary the company offers reflects what the market says you're worth
industry_realities.actual_reality: First offers reflect the company's budget target and what they think they can get away with based on information they have about you; it is not a market clearing price
industry_realities.implication: Treat the first offer as the opening position in a negotiation, not as a take-it-or-leave-it assessment of your value

industry_realities.common_belief: Exploding offer deadlines are genuine signals that the company needs a fast decision
industry_realities.actual_reality: Exploding offers are pressure tactics; virtually every one extends when the candidate explicitly calls it out and requests more time
industry_realities.implication: Never let an exploding offer deadline lapse silently; always request an extension explicitly and frame it around making an informed, committed decision

industry_realities.common_belief: Salary is the main thing you can negotiate, and once it's fixed, negotiation is over
industry_realities.actual_reality: Companies often have far more flexibility on signing bonuses, equity, vacation days, professional development budgets, remote work, and project assignment than on base salary, which is constrained by pay bands and internal equity concerns
industry_realities.implication: When salary hits a ceiling, pivot immediately to other dimensions rather than accepting the ceiling as the end of the conversation

industry_realities.common_belief: Having a competing offer is a form of bluffing or manipulation
industry_realities.actual_reality: Companies rationally respond to competing offers because a single interview is a weak signal of capability; multiple companies making offers is a much stronger combined signal — demand breeds demand
industry_realities.implication: Generating real competing offers through a serious parallel job search is both ethical and strategically essential; it is the most reliable lever available

industry_realities.common_belief: Providing your current salary when asked is a routine step that doesn't affect the outcome
industry_realities.actual_reality: Your current salary becomes the anchor for their offer; a company that knows you earn $70K will offer $75–80K even if they budgeted $95K, because your disclosure has justified underpaying you relative to market
industry_realities.implication: Deflect current salary questions using prepared scripts; the information asymmetry created by not disclosing is directly worth thousands of dollars

industry_realities.common_belief: A small salary increase is too trivial for a company to care about
industry_realities.actual_reality: Employers view employees at 150–200% loaded cost; they think about headcount budget in aggregate, not individual salaries; a $5K salary increase in their budget view is literally 'mouse droppings'
industry_realities.implication: Don't be embarrassed to make requests that seem large to you but are proportionally tiny in the company's budget context

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats the offer as a gift or favor from the employer; feels grateful and reluctant to 'push back' on a company that has chosen them
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats the offer as a mutual market transaction; understands the company has a problem they need solved and has already invested heavily to find the right person
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The mental model determines the entire negotiation posture; gratitude framing produces deference, while transaction framing produces calibrated confidence that recruiters read as competence

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Accepts on the spot or asks for time to think before accepting without countering
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Always counters at least once across at least one dimension before accepting, treating the counter as a standard and expected step
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: First offers are opening positions, not final offers; experienced professionals know this and act accordingly — leaving the first offer unchanged signals either naivety or lack of self-advocacy

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Answers recruiter questions directly and honestly, including current salary and specific salary expectations, treating transparency as a virtue
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Manages information asymmetry deliberately; deflects current salary and specific number questions to preserve optionality, understanding that leverage is a function of what the employer doesn't know
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Premature disclosure collapses negotiating power permanently; information asymmetry is itself a negotiating asset that can be worth thousands of dollars

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Negotiates only on base salary; stops when told salary is fixed
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Maps all negotiable dimensions before entering; when salary hits a ceiling, pivots immediately to signing bonus, equity, vacation, remote work, professional development, and project assignment
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Companies have asymmetric flexibility across comp components; knowing where that flexibility lives means you can gain real value even when the obvious lever is stuck

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats recruiter urgency and exploding deadlines as genuine constraints; accepts 'HR won't approve more' as a final answer
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Recognizes pressure tactics as standard tools; explicitly calls out unrealistic deadlines, mirrors 'HR won't approve' by asking what they can approve on their own
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Pressure tactics only work on people who don't recognize them; naming them neutralizes them and shifts the dynamic back toward collaborative problem-solving

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Lets disappointment or frustration show when numbers are lower than expected; conflates expressing dissatisfaction with creating leverage
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Maintains visible enthusiasm regardless of how disappointing the numbers are; understands that enthusiasm is a negotiating asset and that frustration reduces attractiveness at the worst moment
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Companies deprioritize candidates who seem likely to accept unhappily or leave quickly; enthusiasm signals commitment and keeps the company investing in closing you

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Justifies requests based on personal financial needs — rent, loans, lifestyle expenses — because these feel honest and direct
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Justifies requests using market data and quantified personal impact; personal need is used as a supporting detail at most, not as the primary argument
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Employers are buying labor value, not funding personal expenses; market-based justifications connect to the company's interests and are significantly more persuasive

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Views actively pursuing multiple offers simultaneously as aggressive, disloyal, or manipulative
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats running parallel interview processes as standard professional practice; deliberately sequences interviews to maximize temporal overlap of active offers
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Competing offers are the single most reliable source of leverage; candidates who don't create them are negotiating with much weaker hands than necessary
